questions.question: The focal length of a concave mirror is related to its radius of curvature by:
questions.explanation: $f = R/2$. The focal point is halfway between the pole and the center of curvature.

questions.question: When an object is placed at the center of curvature (C) of a concave mirror, the image is:
questions.explanation: At C, the image is real, inverted, and the same size as the object. It also forms at C.

questions.question: When an object is placed between the focal point (F) and the center of curvature (C) of a concave mirror, the image formed is located:
options: Behind the mirror, virtual, upright, and magnified.
Beyond the center C, real, inverted, and magnified.
At the focal point F, real, inverted, and diminished.
At the center C, real, inverted, and same size.
questions.explanation: When an object is located between F and C of a concave mirror, the reflected light rays converge to form a real, inverted, and magnified image situated further out beyond the center of curvature (C).

questions.question: When an object is at infinity from a concave mirror, the image is formed at:
questions.explanation: Rays from a distant object arrive parallel. A concave mirror converges them at the focal point.

questions.question: When an object is placed between the pole and the focus of a concave mirror, the image is:
questions.explanation: Inside the focal length, a concave mirror acts like a magnifying glass — virtual, upright, and magnified.

questions.question: A concave mirror is used as a shaving mirror because when the face is placed close to the mirror, it produces a:
questions.explanation: With the face inside the focal length, the mirror produces a magnified, upright, virtual image for detailed viewing.

questions.question: When parallel rays of light strike a concave mirror parallel to the principal axis, they converge at the:
questions.explanation: The focal point is where parallel rays converge after reflection from a concave mirror.

questions.question: A concave mirror has a radius of curvature of $40\text{ cm}$. What is its focal length?
A real image is one that:
options: Can be projected onto a screen
Cannot be projected onto a screen
Is always larger than the object
questions.explanation: Real images are formed where light rays actually converge. They can be captured on a screen.

questions.question: When an object is placed at the focal point of a concave mirror, the image is formed:
questions.explanation: Rays from an object at F reflect parallel to the principal axis. They never converge, so the image is at infinity.

questions.question: A dentist uses a small concave mirror to examine teeth. The tooth is placed within the focal length, so the dentist sees a:
questions.explanation: With the tooth inside F, the mirror produces a magnified virtual image — exactly what the dentist needs.

questions.question: Concave mirrors are highly effective in solar furnaces because they:
options: Spread incoming sunlight over a vastly wide surface area.
Concentrate parallel light rays onto a single focal point.
Produce upright virtual images of the moving sun overhead.
Reflect intense light rays outwards in all possible directions.
questions.explanation: Because the sun is incredibly far away, its incoming light rays arrive at Earth parallel to each other. When these parallel rays hit a large concave mirror, they reflect and converge at a single, intense focal point, creating the extreme temperatures needed to power a solar furnace.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered concave mirrors and image formation!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review image characteristics when the object is between F and C, and inside F.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $f = R/2$ and the six cases of object position for concave mirrors.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: focal point, center of curvature, real vs. virtual images, and ray diagrams.
